text:
Epilepsy Centre 

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Patel  
St George's University Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust  
Neurology Department, Blackshaw Road, London SW17 0QT  

Dear Dr Patel,  

Michael Turner, DOB: 14-07-1988, Hospital No: H529734 NHS No. 9074512368  
Flat 3, 21 Brookside Close, London, SW12 0AB  

Diagnoses: 

Focal epilepsy with impaired awareness seizures (right temporal lobe onset suspected). 

I reviewed Michael Turner in clinic today. He reports a long-standing history of focal seizures characterised by a rising epigastric sensation, brief behavioural arrest, oroalimentary automatisms and post-ictal disorientation lasting 5–10 minutes. Over the last two months he reports TC X9/wk, which he describes as typical focal events without generalisation. He denies convulsive activity. He works at height with powered tools and moving plant in a manual/safety-sensitive role; he states that he has informed his line manager and is currently on restricted duties away from ladders and machinery pending occupational health review. 

Current anti-seizure medication: lamotrigine 150 mg twice daily and levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. He reports full adherence, no recent dose changes, and only mild irritability on levetiracetam. No rescue medication has been issued and none used. There have been no emergency attendances, head injuries or status events. Sleep is fragmented due to early shifts; alcohol intake is minimal; no recreational drug use. 

Examination today was unremarkable with normal speech, cranial nerves, coordination and gait. Prior MRI (2023) showed subtle right mesial temporal T2/FLAIR signal change without definite volume loss; EEG (2024) demonstrated right temporal interictal sharp waves. Bloods in May 2025 (U&E, LFT, FBC) were within reference ranges. 

Plan: 
- Increase lamotrigine to 200 mg twice daily over two weeks if tolerated, maintaining levetiracetam 1000 mg twice daily. Patient provided with a written titration schedule and advised to report rash or mood change promptly. 
- Arrange repeat EEG with sleep deprivation and update MRI epilepsy protocol to reassess right mesial temporal structures. 
- Occupational health referral requested today to support continuation of restricted duties given ongoing seizures (TC X9/wk) in a safety-sensitive environment. Patient will avoid working at heights and with moving machinery as agreed with employer; he confirms this arrangement is already in place. 
- Provide seizure first-aid information to employer, and signpost to patient information on triggers and sleep hygiene. 
- DVLA guidance discussed; he confirms he is not currently driving and understands to notify the DVLA if circumstances change. 

We will review in 10–12 weeks or sooner if seizure pattern changes, particularly if there is any generalisation, injury, or medication adverse effects. 

Yours sincerely

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states the patient's current seizure frequency using the notation 'TC X9/wk' in two places (history and plan). The context clarifies these are typical focal seizures without generalisation. Interpreting 'X9/wk' as 9 per week. No other frequencies are provided. Apply the highest frequency rule: 9 per week. Convert to allowed format: '9 per week'.
